meta.subject: physics
meta.topic_name: Motion of Two Bodies One on Other

intro.kicker: Topic 12 of 38 • Chapter: Friction • Unit: Mechanics • Physics
intro.topicName: Motion of Two Bodies One on Other
intro.description: When body A (mass m) rests on body B (mass M), and a force F is applied to either body, four distinct cases arise depending on whether friction is absent, below limiting value, or above limiting value. When F < μs mg (limiting friction between A and B), both bodies move together with common acceleration F/(M+m). When F > μs mg, bodies move separately with kinetic friction coupling them. A further complication arises when there is also friction between B and the floor. NEET tests all four cases across both force configurations (force on upper body A and force on lower body B).

expectedQuestions.description: High-frequency NEET topic testing Newton’s second law in coupled-system problems. Typical patterns: (i) find common acceleration and friction force when F < limiting; (ii) find individual accelerations when F > limiting; (iii) find maximum F for combined motion; (iv) determine when body A falls off body B. Appears in most NEET papers under Laws of Motion or Friction.

timeRequired.description: 1.5 hr on 8 cases (4 for force on A, 4 for force on B), 1.5 hr on past MCQs. This topic has the highest case count in the Friction chapter. Recommended strategy: tabulate all 8 sub-cases with acceleration formulas and friction directions before attempting MCQs. Common acceleration (F < Fl) and individual accelerations with kinetic friction (F > Fl) must be derived confidently.

difficulty.description: Most complex topic in the Friction chapter: 8 sub-cases, multiple friction surfaces, and relative motion analysis. NEET traps: (i) always check if the system moves at all (check F vs. limiting friction between B and floor); (ii) when force is on lower body B and F < μs(m+M)g, both bodies move together; (iii) pseudo-force method gives the threshold condition for combined motion when force is on B. Drawing free-body diagrams for both A and B separately is mandatory.

nriUsaGap.description: AP Physics covers two-body problems but not the systematic case analysis with 4+ sub-cases. NEET requires students to identify which case applies (no friction, F < Fl, F > Fl, friction on floor too) and compute accelerations for each. The pseudo-force approach for determining the threshold when force is applied to the lower body is not part of AP Physics scope.

weightage.sectionTitle: Motion of Two Bodies One on Other — NEET Weightage Analysis (2019–2024)
bulletNotes: 1–2 questions per year; consistent appearance across all recent NEET papers.
Case of combined vs. separate motion (F < vs. > limiting friction) is the core test.
Maximum force for combined motion F_max = μs(M+m)g is a frequently computed quantity.

strategySteps.title: Master the Two Combined-Motion Conditions
strategySteps.description: Force on upper body A: combined motion if F < μs mg. Force on lower body B: combined motion if F < μs(m+M)g. Both conditions must be instant recalls. The limiting friction between A and B (= μs mg) governs the A-on-top case; the limiting friction between B and floor governs the B-case threshold.

strategySteps.title: Draw Separate FBDs for A and B
strategySteps.description: For F > limiting: draw FBD for A and B separately. Friction between A and B (= μk mg) is the only horizontal force on A (in A-on-top case); for B it appears as a force in the direction of F. This gives aA = μk g and aB = (F − μk mg)/M.

strategySteps.title: Check Status of B-Floor Friction Before Solving
strategySteps.description: If the problem mentions friction between B and floor (μ'), first check if B moves at all: B moves only if kinetic friction coupling from A exceeds B-floor limiting friction. If B stays, static friction on B-floor adjusts to match. Never assume B moves.

cards.title: Two-Body Stacked System Notes
cards.description: All 8 sub-cases with FBD diagrams, acceleration formulas, combined-motion conditions, and 15 NEET-pattern MCQs covering force on upper and lower body configurations.

cards.title: Case Table: A on B System
cards.description: Quick-reference table for all cases: friction condition, which body moves, individual accelerations, friction force on each body, and fall-time formula when A slides off B.

subtopicsIndex.sectionTitle: Subtopics in Motion of Two Bodies One on Other

cards.title: Force on Upper Body A — Four Cases
cards.coreRevision: Case (i): No friction. aA = F/m; aB = 0. Case (ii): Friction A-B only, F < Fl. Both move together: a = F/(M+m). Case (iii): Friction A-B only, F > Fl. Bodies separate: aA = (F − μk mg)/m; aB = μk mg/M. Case (iv): Friction on B-floor too. B moves only if kinetic friction from A on B exceeds μ'(M+m)g.
points: Combined motion condition (force on A): F < μs mg
Separate: aA = (F − μk mg)/m; aB = μk mg/M
Friction on A when F > Fl: backwards (opposing relative motion of A on B)
Friction on B from A: forwards (in direction A tends to move relative to B)
cards.example: m = 2 kg, M = 3 kg, μs = 0.4, μk = 0.3, g = 10 m/s². Fl = μs mg = 8 N. If F = 6 N < 8 N: a = 6/5 = 1.2 m/s² (both together). If F = 15 N > 8 N: aA = (15−6)/(2) = 4.5 m/s²; aB = 6/3 = 2 m/s².

cards.title: Force on Lower Body B — Four Cases
cards.coreRevision: Case (i): No friction. aB = F/M; aA = 0 (remains at rest relative to ground). Case (ii): Friction A-B only, F < μs(M+m)g. Both move together: a = F/(M+m). Case (iii): Friction A-B only, F > μs mg. Bodies separate: aA = μk g; aB = (F − μk mg)/M. Case (iv): Friction on B-floor too: B moves only if F > μk(m+M)g.
points: Combined motion condition (force on B): F < μs(M+m)g
Combined acceleration: a = F/(M+m) (same formula as force-on-A case)
Separate (F > Fl): aA = μk g; aB = (F − μk mg)/M
When B is accelerated rapidly under A, A lags behind — friction from B pulls A forward
Pseudo-force on A (in B’s frame) = ma = mF/(M+m) < μs mg for combined motion
cards.example: m = 2 kg, M = 5 kg, μs = 0.3, μk = 0.25, g = 10 m/s². Combined condition: F < 0.3×2×10 = 6 N? No: threshold = μs(m+M)g = 0.3×7×10 = 21 N. If F = 15 N: both move together, a = 15/7 ≈ 2.14 m/s². If F = 30 N: aA = μk g = 2.5 m/s²; aB = (30−5)/(5) = 5 m/s².

curriculum.hintText: How AP Physics and US curricula compare to NEET scope on stacked-body friction problems.

gaps.title: Systematic Case Analysis Not Required in AP Physics
gaps.description: AP Physics tests one or two friction setups in a single problem. NEET requires knowing 8 sub-cases (4 for force on A, 4 for force on B) and determining which case applies from given data. Students from US curricula should practise categorising the sub-case before computing accelerations.
bullets: Identify friction condition: none / F < Fl / F > Fl / floor friction present.
Apply combined or separate motion equations for each sub-case.

gaps.title: Fall-Time Formula When A Slides Off B
gaps.description: When F > Fl (A slides on B), A will eventually fall off B after time t = √(2L/a_rel), where L = length of B and a_rel = aA − aB is relative acceleration of A w.r.t. B. This formula is NEET-specific; AP Physics does not test it.
bullets: Relative acceleration a_rel = aA − aB when force is on A.
Fall time t = √(2L/a_rel) derived from s = ½at².

questions.question: A block A of mass 3 kg is placed on block B of mass 7 kg. Coefficient of static friction between A and B is 0.3. What is the maximum horizontal force that can be applied to B so that both blocks move together? (g = 10 m/s², floor is frictionless)
questions.explanation: For combined motion with force on lower body B (frictionless floor): F_max = μs(m+M)g = 0.3×(3+7)×10 = 0.3×100 = 30 N. This is derived from pseudo-force condition: mF/(m+M) < μs mg ⇒ F < μs(m+M)g.

questions.question: Block A (2 kg) lies on block B (5 kg) on a frictionless floor. μs(A,B) = 0.4, μk(A,B) = 0.3. A force of 20 N is applied to A. g = 10 m/s². Acceleration of B is:
questions.explanation: Fl = μs mg = 0.4×2×10 = 8 N. F = 20 N > 8 N, so bodies move separately. Friction on B from A = μk mg = 0.3×2×10 = 6 N (in direction of F). aB = 6/5 = 1.2 m/s². Meanwhile aA = (20−6)/2 = 7 m/s².

questions.question: Block A (3 kg) on block B (7 kg), frictionless floor, μs(A-B) = 0.2, g = 10 m/s². Force 18 N on A. Do they move together?
options: Yes, Fl = 6 N < 18 N so combined
No, F > Fl = 6 N; they move separately
questions.answer: No, F > Fl = 6 N; they move separately

questions.question: When no friction exists between A and B (A on B, force F on A), the acceleration of B is:
For force applied to lower body B, the maximum force for combined motion equals:
When F > limiting friction between A and B, and force is on A, friction force on B from A acts in which direction?

items.title: State combined motion condition for both force configurations
items.description: Force on A: F < μs mg for combined motion. Force on B: F < μs(m+M)g for combined motion. Different thresholds — do not confuse them.

items.title: Write acceleration formulas for separate motion
items.description: Force on A (F > Fl): aA = (F − μk mg)/m; aB = μk mg/M. Force on B (F > Fl): aA = μk g; aB = (F − μk mg)/M. Know which friction term goes where on each body's FBD.

items.title: Check floor friction before moving B
items.description: If friction between B and floor is present, B moves only if force driving B > limiting friction (B-floor). When B is stationary, friction between B and floor = force driving B (self-adjusting static friction).

items.title: Compute fall-time when A slides off B
items.description: Relative acceleration a_rel = |aA − aB|; fall time t = √(2L/a_rel) where L = length of B. This gives time for A to completely slide off B.

items.question: Why does the combined motion threshold differ for force on A vs. force on B?
items.answer: When force F acts on A (upper body), the only friction that can accelerate B is the friction between A and B. This friction ≤ μs mg. So for B to keep up with A: μs mg must be enough to give B the same acceleration, which requires F < μs mg total (not just for B). When force F acts on B, the friction between A and B must accelerate A: required friction = mF/(M+m). For combined motion: mF/(M+m) < μs mg ⇒ F < μs(M+m)g.

items.question: What is friction force on B when F > limiting (force applied to A, no floor friction)?
items.answer: When A slides on B (F > Fl), kinetic friction between A and B = μk mg acts backward on A (opposing A's forward sliding) and forward on B (Newton's third law pair). So friction on B = μk mg in the direction of applied force F. This accelerates B: aB = μk mg/M.

items.question: If there is friction between B and the floor, when does B remain stationary?
items.answer: B is accelerated by friction from A, which at most equals μk mg (kinetic friction between A and B). B stays stationary if this μk mg ≤ μ'(M+m)g (limiting friction between B and floor). When B is stationary, static friction from floor adjusts to balance exactly the force transmitted to B. B moves only if μk mg > μ'(M+m)g.

items.question: Can the block A ever slide backward off B?
items.answer: Yes — when force is applied to B (lower body) and F > μs mg, B accelerates more than A, so relative to B, A moves backward and eventually falls off the rear end of B. Fall time = √(2L/a_rel) where a_rel = aB − aA = (F − μk mg)/M − μk g is the relative retardation of A w.r.t. B.

grounding_summary: Pages 241–242 (textbook pp. 228–229) systematically cover all four sub-cases for force on upper body A: no friction, F < Fl (combined motion), F > Fl (separate motion with kinetic friction coupling), and friction on B-floor. Then all four sub-cases for force on lower body B follow.

The textbook derives combined acceleration a = F/(M+m) for both force configurations and gives kinetic friction as the coupling force when bodies move separately, with individual acceleration expressions.

Page 242 also covers the floor-friction case for force on B: B moves only if kinetic friction from A exceeds limiting friction between B and floor; otherwise static friction on B-floor adjusts to balance.
grounding_excerpt: static friction will work (not limiting friction) between body
Both the body will move with different acceleration.

sources.title: NCERT Physics Class 11 Part 1 — Chapter 5: Laws of Motion (Friction and Coupled Bodies)
sources.why_used: Primary NCERT source establishing the two-body stacked friction analysis with all four sub-cases and Newton's second law applications to coupled systems.

sources.title: NEET Physics Friction MCQs Set A with Answers — StudiesToday
sources.why_used: Verified NEET MCQ patterns for two-body friction problems including maximum force for combined motion and acceleration of individual bodies.

review.notes: Content grounded on pages 240-242 OCR. All 8 sub-cases (4 per force configuration) covered per textbook scope. Combined motion thresholds derived correctly for both configurations.
